sources.title: mini-swe-agent v2.4.5
sources.why_selected: It proves that most of the value can come from model quality, sandboxing, and clear transcripts rather than elaborate agent frameworks.
sources.usefulness: Use as a readable baseline for Buildn issue-fixing jobs, quick repo triage, and client-facing demos of inspectable automation.

sources.title: SWE-bench leaderboards + CodeClash
sources.why_selected: It gives Abdelrahman a neutral way to compare models and scaffolds before standardizing a coding-agent stack.
sources.usefulness: Make a monthly Buildn coding-agent eval sheet using mini-swe-agent as the fixed harness and only vary models/costs.

sources.title: OpenCode desktop beta + terminal agent
sources.why_selected: It is the clearest OSS alternative to proprietary coding-agent UX while keeping model choice flexible.
sources.usefulness: Pilot as the default terminal coding UI for non-sensitive client repos and as a shared session artifact for code review.

sources.title: Cline open-source agent
sources.why_selected: It is useful where the developer wants visual review and explicit planning before writes.
sources.usefulness: Use for client repo migrations where PMs or junior engineers need to inspect proposed changes before acting.

sources.title: Continue open-source coding agent
sources.why_selected: It fits teams that want source-controlled assistants and enforceable AI checks instead of opaque IDE magic.
sources.usefulness: Create Buildn-specific assistant configs for PR review, docs upkeep, and repo onboarding.

sources.title: Roo Code multi-mode agent
sources.why_selected: Mode separation is useful for keeping architecture discussion, implementation, and debugging from collapsing into one noisy thread.
sources.usefulness: Borrow the mode pattern for Buildn OS agent recipes: architect, implementer, verifier, docs editor.

sources.title: Google Marketing Live 2026: Ask Advisor + AI Search ads
sources.why_selected: Search campaigns are becoming conversational, assistant-mediated, and measurement-heavy; old keyword-only playbooks will miss demand.
sources.usefulness: Build a client audit: AI Search appearance, creative asset readiness, conversion measurement, and experiments to run in Google Ads.

sources.title: Ahrefs Brand Radar
sources.why_selected: It turns answer-engine visibility from anecdotal prompt checks into a repeatable competitive research workflow.
sources.usefulness: Run Buildn and client competitors through Brand Radar to find missing citations, Reddit/YouTube surfaces, and AEO content targets.

sources.title: Semrush AI Visibility Toolkit
sources.why_selected: It is a lower-cost on-ramp for agencies that already use Semrush and need AI-search reporting.
sources.usefulness: Package a lightweight 'AI visibility baseline' deliverable for client retainers: prompts, competitors, blockers, and actions.

sources.title: HubSpot Breeze AI Agents
sources.why_selected: It shows CRM agents maturing into configurable teammates that work inside approved business data rather than standalone chatbots.
sources.usefulness: Map Buildn's sales flow into agent candidates: company research, lead enrichment, follow-up draft, and customer-health summaries.

sources.title: Clay as GTM orchestration layer
sources.why_selected: It remains one of the most practical places to chain AI, data vendors, and human review for outbound without building custom infrastructure.
sources.usefulness: Create a Buildn target-account workflow: scrape signals, enrich contacts, summarize pains, draft personalized first-touch, sync CRM.

sources.title: Product Hunt growth tooling: SEORCE + Inrō
sources.why_selected: The small-tool market is converging on two practical jobs: AI-era discoverability and conversational lead capture.
sources.usefulness: Use SEORCE-style workflows for client visibility audits; use Inrō-style DM flows for founder-led community funnels.

sources.title: Mem0 State of AI Agent Memory 2026
sources.why_selected: It frames memory as measurable infrastructure rather than 'just add a vector DB'.
sources.usefulness: Use its evaluation dimensions to audit Hermes/Buildn memory: recall, update handling, temporal reasoning, cost, latency, stale fact cleanup.

sources.title: MemOS 2.0 Stardust
sources.why_selected: It gives a concrete architecture for persistent agent memory beyond raw embedding retrieval.
sources.usefulness: Prototype a local Buildn memory layer for client/project facts, agent traces, decisions, and reusable skills.

sources.title: MemoryAgentBench at ICLR 2026
sources.why_selected: It tests the failure modes that real assistants hit after weeks of use: conflicts, stale facts, and multi-session recall.
sources.usefulness: Adapt a mini benchmark for Buildn OS: inject project history once, query it many times, then score recall and conflict behavior.

sources.title: Beyond Memorization: AI chatbots for smarter retrieval practice
sources.why_selected: It connects classic retrieval-practice evidence to practical AI tutoring workflows.
sources.usefulness: Turn Abdelrahman's notes into daily AI-generated recall prompts, not summaries; score answers and schedule weak concepts.

sources.title: FSRS / open-spaced-repetition
sources.why_selected: It remains the practical algorithmic backbone for durable personal learning.
sources.usefulness: Use FSRS as the scheduling layer for AI-generated flashcards from books, papers, client lessons, and codebase concepts.

sources.title: Obsidian as local PKM substrate
sources.why_selected: Local markdown remains the simplest durable substrate for memory systems that should outlive a vendor.
sources.usefulness: Keep Buildn/learning notes in markdown, then layer RAG, spaced repetition, and agent memory on top.
